title: Brokers versus Retail Investors: Conflicting Interests and Dominated Products
abstract: I study how brokers distort household investment decisions. Using a novel convertible bond data set, I find that consumers often purchase dominated bonds—cheap and expensive otherwise-identical bonds coexist in the market. Brokers are incentivized to sell the dominated bonds, typically earning two times greater fees for selling them. I develop and estimate a broker-intermediated search model that rationalizes this behavior. The estimates indicate that costly search is a key friction in financial markets, but the effects of search costs are compounded when brokers are incentivized to direct the search of consumers toward high-fee inferior products.

article.introduction:
The prices and fees of seeminglyidentical financial products often differ drastically. Previous research documents price heterogeneity across mutual funds, mortgages, bonds, and other financial products.1What drives this behavior? Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004) highlight the importance of search in a consumer's investment decision process. But consumer search does not happen in a vacuum. Broker intermediation plays a critical role in a consumer's investment decision and search process. In 2010, 56% of American households sought investment advice from a financial professional (Survey of Consumer Finances [available athttps://www.federalreserve.gov/econres/scfindex.htm]). Despite their prevalence, however, brokers—commonly referred to as financial advisors—may not be acting in the best interest of their clients. Brokers in the United States are not held to a fiduciary standard, which means that they are not legally obligated to act in a client's best financial interests. A broker may therefore legally choose to subordinate his client's interests to his own financial interests by directing his client to inferior products with high brokerage fees. Former President Barack Obama (2015) called for regulators to “update the rules and requirements that retirement advisors put the best interests of their clients above their own financial interests. It's a very simple principle: You want to give financial advice, you've got to put your client's interests first.” Using a novel data set that precisely measures the conflicts of interest between brokers and consumers in a clean setting, in this paper I illustrate how conflicts of interest in the brokerage industry distort investment decisions and compound search/information frictions in retail financial markets.
The paper has two goals. The first is to use a unique clean setting to show that brokers distort the investment decisions of consumers. I show that brokers play a critical role in determining consumers' investment outcomes and are incentivized to sell inferior products. Accounting for the incentives of financial intermediaries is of first-order importance in understanding investment flows for the financial products studied in this paper. The second goal is to rationalize the behavior of brokers and consumers in equilibrium by developing and estimating a broker-intermediated search model. In the model, brokers facilitate consumers' search process by selecting financial products to offer to each consumer. In equilibrium brokers maximize expected brokerage commissions by selecting products based on product quality (risk and return), commissions, and the consumer's level of sophistication.2I then use the model to quantify the distortions in the market due to heterogeneity in consumer sophistication, search costs, and broker incentives.3
Several data challenges complicate investigating the above questions. First, data on how brokers are compensated are scarce. The product studied most extensively in the literature are mutual funds, but there are limited data on how brokers are compensated for selling mutual funds. Mutual funds report 12b-1 fees, which are used to cover aggregate marketing, distributional, and service expenses, but 12b-1 fees do not tell us how brokers/intermediaries are specifically compensated. Additionally, since mutual fund fees are paid by the investor, a change in 12b-1 fees directly impacts the incentives of both brokers and consumers, which complicates identification.4I address these challenges by constructing a new retail bond data set. In the data set broker compensation comes in the form of a “kick-back” that is paid by the bond issuer to the broker. Because consumers do not pay the kick-back, conditional on the other product characteristics, consumers are indifferent to the commission/kick-back. This unique feature of the data allows me to separately identify the preferences of brokers and consumers.
Second, a related challenge is that it can be difficult to directly compare and establish a definitive rank ordering of financial products. Financial products, such as mutual funds, vary across several observable and unobservable dimensions, making direct comparisons of products challenging. For example, we may think that Vanguard's S&P 500 fund, which charges 0.17% a year, is better than Guggenheim's Rydex S&P 500 fund, which charges 2.28% a year, but product differentiation and preference heterogeneity make such claims tenuous (Del Guercio and Reuter (2014)).5Moreover, attempting to draw any inference from variation in fees across funds is confounded by other product characteristics. Fees/commissions are endogenous and are almost certainly correlated with the unobserved product characteristics of the funds. I construct a new bond data set in which the products are relatively simple and are characterized completely by a few observable features so that a rank ordering of financial products is indeed possible.
I address these data challenges by constructing a new retail bond data set covering all one-year reverse convertible bonds issued in the United States over the period 2008 to 2012. A reverse convertible is similar to a standard fixed-rate bond in that it pays fixed monthly/quarterly coupons based on the initial investment, but differs in that the final principal payment is convertible into shares of some prespecified reference equity. At maturity, bondholders receive either their full principal or a fixed number of shares of the reference equity if the reference equity share price falls below some predetermined convertible threshold. Reverse convertibles are typically purchased by wealthy individuals and households with incomes in excess of $100,000, liquid assets in excess of $100,000, and total net worth of at least $250,000.6
The advantage of studying reverse convertible bonds over other financial products is twofold. First, reverse convertibles are characterized completely by a small number of dimensions, namely, a fixed coupon and an equity-linked principal payment. As a result, it is easy to locate simultaneously issued reverse convertibles for which the payout of one reverse convertible is unambiguously dominated by another—the bond with the higher coupon. Consider two nearly identical one-year reverse convertibles issued by JPMorgan Chase on June 30, 2008.7One reverse convertible pays a fixed coupon of 11.25%, whereas the other pays a fixed coupon of 9.00%. Both reverse convertibles were sold to investors at a fixed par price of 100%. The final principal payment of both reverse convertibles is identical and linked to the share price of Microsoft Inc. If the share price of Microsoft Inc. remains above the predetermined convertible trigger price of $22.68 over the life of the bond, investors receive their full principal back at maturity and earn returns of 11.25% and 9.00%. If the price of Microsoft Inc. shares ever closes below $22.68, the bond principal (for both bonds) is converted into equity such that at maturity bondholders receive 35.27 shares of Microsoft Inc. for every $1,000 invested.8Figure1displays the hypothetical return to investors of the two products if the principal is converted as a function of the price of Microsoft Inc. at maturity. Once the principal is converted into shares of Microsoft Inc., the payoff of both convertible bonds is a decreasing function of the price of Microsoft Inc. and resembles a short equity put option. Notice that the return of the 11.25% reverse convertible clearly dominates that of the 9.00% reverse convertible. There are no minimum quantity, selling, or issuance restrictions on either bond, and thus both bonds were available to consumers. Although all consumers would always be better off purchasing the superior 11.25% reverse convertible and could have done so, consumers purchased more than 10 times as much of the 9% dominated product. This example of a bank simultaneously issuing a dominated/superior product is not unique—I observe over 100 dominated/superior reverse convertibles in the data set.
The second advantage of studying reverse convertibles is that the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) requires all bond issuers to disclose the fees/commissions paid to brokers. Reverse convertible bond issuers, rather than consumers, compensate brokers with fees/kick-backs for selling reverse convertibles. In the previous JPMorgan example, JPMorgan paid brokers a fee of 3.09% relative to the notional invested for selling the worse 9.00% reverse convertible and only 2.15% for selling the better reverse convertible.9The fees/kick-backs do not measure differences in advice or bundled services provided by brokers.10The data therefore allow me to quantify the degree to which broker incentives influence consumer choice.
The first part of the paper reveals three stylized empirical facts. First, the market value of reverse convertibles exhibits substantial dispersion and consumers often fail to purchase the best available financial product. The standard deviation of the market value in the data set is over 2.40% relative to the notional invested. Second, when better and worse products are available, consumers actually tend to purchase more of the worse product. Third, the evidence suggests that the incentives of brokers do not align with the incentives of consumers. All else equal, consumers tend to purchase more products with higher commissions/kick-backs, and products with higher commissions tend to have lower payoffs.11I argue that the incentive and information asymmetry between brokers and consumers helps rationalize product issuance and the behavior of consumers.
In the second part of the paper, I rationalize the behavior of brokers and consumers in equilibrium by developing and estimating a model of financial distribution. In the model, consumers search for investment products with the aid of brokers. The key novel feature of the model is the role of brokers: brokers act as intermediaries by selecting products to offer each consumer based on the quality of financial products and associated commissions. In other words, broker profit maximization endogenously determines the distribution of products that consumers observe. Consumers ultimately decide whether to purchase the offered product. Consumers differ in their level of financial sophistication/search costs, and brokers utilize the full product space to price discriminate across consumers based on the consumer's level of financial sophistication. Brokers sell the low-fee superior reverse convertibles to sophisticated consumers and dominated high-fee reverse convertibles to unsophisticated consumers.
The model provides sharp insights that are useful in understanding consumer and broker behavior beyond the reverse convertible market. A key feature of the data is that consumers not only buy inferior financial products, but they actually purchase more of the inferior product relative to the superior product. A standard random search model or a model with heterogeneous consumer sophistication can help explain why consumers with high search costs or a low level of sophistication might buy inferior products, but cannot help explain why consumers purchase more of them. A standard random search model would predict the exact opposite: consumers should purchase more of the superior product.22I therefore introduce the second friction, namely, broker-intermediated search. Brokers are incentivized to show high-fee products, which makes finding better products relative to worse products potentially harder for consumers. The reason both better and worse products exist is that such product variation gives brokers the ability to price/quality discriminate across consumers based on the consumer's search cost/sophistication. Broker's incentives together with consumer search help explain why consumers generally fail to purchase the best available products.
I use the empirical model to quantify the distortions in these markets: search costs and broker/consumer conflicts of interest/agency frictions. The model estimates suggest that the average searching consumer spends roughly $14 per search or $30 total (in terms of the cost of time and effort) searching for a $1,000 investment. Brokers are often twice as likely to market an inferior product (relative to the superior product) to unsophisticated consumers. While both frictions are economically meaningful, it is the interaction between the two that is particularly important in retail financial markets.
I investigate two partial-equilibrium counterfactuals that quantitatively illustrate how conflicts of interest in the brokerage industry exacerbate existing informational and search frictions. First, I examine a counterfactual whereby consumers randomly search across products without the aid of brokers, similar to the underlying assumption in Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004), while keeping the product space fixed. Allowing consumers to randomly sample the product space resolves the conflict of interest problem but does not result in the most efficient search process for consumers. The model estimates suggest that if consumers randomly searched the product space, the returns of consumers would increase by a modest 5 to 21 basis points (bp). Although resolving the conflict of interest problem helps consumers, it does not resolve the fundamental friction in the market, that is, costly search. Second, I examine the counterfactual whereby brokers are forced to show consumers the best available products while keeping the product space fixed. This counterfactual resolves the conflict of interest problem and allows consumers to search the product space more efficiently. When the incentives of brokers and consumers are aligned, brokers alleviate the search frictions in these markets. The model suggests that the returns of consumers would increase by 95 to 120 bp if we were to align the incentives of brokers and consumers. The empirical results show that costly search/heterogeneous consumer sophistication, conflicts of interest, and, most importantly, the interaction between the two are of first-order importance in terms of determining consumers' investment outcomes.
This paper relates to the economics and finance literature regarding price and quality dispersion in financial products. Previous work including Massa (2000), Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004), and Choi, Laibson, and Madrian (2010) indicates that the Law of One Price may fail to hold in the mutual fund industry. Anagol, Cole, and Sakar (2017) find similar evidence in life insurance markets in India. One limitation of previous studies is that much of the observed dispersion in the prices and quality of financial products could potentially be rationalized by unobserved product characteristics and preference heterogeneity. This paper offers the cleanest setting for studying retail financial markets. All consumers would be unambiguously better off purchasing the superior reverse convertible over the dominated convertible regardless of the consumer's preferences or portfolio holdings.
This paper also relates to the growing literature on structured products. Reverse convertibles are often synonymous with structured products, which are often criticized for their opaqueness and high costs (Stoimenov and Wilkens (2005), Bergstresser (2008), Szymanowska, Ter Horst, and Veld (2009), and Henderson and Pearson (2011)). The paper also relates to Carlin (2009), Carlin and Manso (2011), Gennaioli, Shleifer, and Vishny (2012), and Célérier and Vallée (2017) regarding financial innovation.12
Researchers have documented the potential broker and consumer information and incentive asymmetry arising in consumer finance (Livingston and O'Neal (1996), Mahoney (2004), Bolton, Freixas, and Shapiro (2007), Bergstresser, Chalmers, and Tufano (2009), Woodward and Hall (2012), Christoffersen, Evans, and Musto (2013)). I find evidence consistent with Bergstresser, Chalmers, and Tufano (2009), Hackethal, Inderst, and Myer (2012), Anagol, Cole, and Sakar (2017), Christoffersen, Evans, and Musto (2013), and Chalmers and Reuter (2015) suggesting that brokers direct consumers to high-fee products. This paper builds on the preceding work by studying the distribution of financial products in a clean and novel setting. In the data set, I observe all product characteristics as well as the fees paid to brokers. By directly comparing dominated and superior products, I can isolate the effect of broker fees on product issuance. Previous research suggests that the underlying economic frictions in the market for reverse convertibles apply to a much broader set of financial markets.
The rest of the paper is structured as follows. SectionIdescribes the institutional background and data. SectionIIreports the main empirical results. SectionIIIpresents my structural model of financial distribution, and SectionIVdescribes the corresponding estimation strategy and results. SectionVexplores two counterfactuals that illustrate how conflicts of interest in the brokerage industry interact with existing information and search frictions. Finally, SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Background: Reverse Con:
I study the market for equity reverse convertible bonds. A standard fixed-rate bond consists of a set of fixed coupon payments and a final principal payment at maturity. Reverse convertible bonds are the same as fixed-rate bonds with one notable exception: the final principal payment of reverse convertibles can be converted into shares of a prespecified reference equity. The reference equity is different from the bond issuer. In the previous example in Figure1, JPMorgan Chase issued a reverse convertible linked to shares of Microsoft. Each reverse convertible has a prespecified reference equity and convertible trigger price. At maturity, investors receive 100% of their principal, provided that the underlying reference equity remains above the prespecified convertible trigger price. If the equity falls below the convertible trigger price during the life of the bond, investors receive a fixed number of equity shares rather than the full principal amount. The fixed number of shares the investor receives is equal to the initial principal investment divided by the reference equity share price on the date the bond is issued. Thus, if the share price of the reference equity falls over the life of the bond, the value of the shares the investor receives at maturity may be worth substantially less than the initial principal amount invested.
In practice, two common types of reverse convertibles exist: single-observation and continuous-observation reverse convertibles. The two types of reverse convertibles differ in terms of when/how the bond principal is converted into equity. The previous discussion and the example in Figure1describe a continuous-observation reverse convertible. For a continuous-observation reverse convertible, the principal is converted into equity if the price of the prespecified reference equityevercloses below the trigger price. For a single-observation reverse convertible, the principal is converted into equity if the reference equity price is below the convertible trigger priceat maturityrather than if the equity price is ever below the convertible trigger price.
A reverse convertible essentially combines a standard fixed-rate bond and an equity put option into one financial product. By buying a continuous- (single-) observation reverse convertible, the bondholder effectively holds a long position in a fixed-rate bond and is short a knock-in (standard) European put option. As illustrated in Figure1, the bondholder is short a Microsoft Inc. knock-in put option that is struck at the initial share price of $28.35 and knocks in at the convertible trigger price of $22.68. An alternative way to think of a reverse convertible is that it is a vehicle for investors to sell equity put options to issuers. Rather than directly receiving the option premium, investors instead receive relatively high coupons on the accompanying fixed-rate bond.
Reverse convertibles offer a unique setting for understanding consumer investments and studying retail financial distribution. The financial industry largely views reverse convertibles as the “Gold Standard” of retail structured products. In 2008, banks issued roughly $10 billion of reverse convertibles in the United States and over $50 billion globally, the bulk of which were purchased by retail investors.13As a point of reference, sales for fixed income annuities, a popular retirement product in the United States, were $67 billion in 2007 and $107 billion in 2008.14Reverse convertibles are largely an access product, which allows purchasers to sell equity options/volatility, and thus makes these products desirable for retail consumers rather than for companies and professional investors. Reverse convertibles provide investors with an opportunity to earn a relatively high return on a standard three-month to two-year fixed-rate bond by taking additional equity risk.15From the issuing bank's perspective, reverse convertibles provide the bank with an opportunity to purchase equity options from retail consumers without having to worry about counterparty risk. In other words, reverse convertibles provide consumers with a vehicle to sell equity options without a margin account. To protect retail consumers, the SEC requires disclosure of the details of each reverse convertible issued, including the fee/kick-back the issuer pays to the broker. Though relatively simple, the complexity and prevalence of reverse convertibles makes them of particular importance when analyzing retail financial markets.
The reverse convertible market consists of three players: product issuers, brokers, and retail consumers. Product issuers, typically investment banks, create and issue reverse convertibles. Brokers purchase reverse convertible bonds from the product issuer and then sell the bonds to retail consumers.
Figure2illustrates the reverse convertible distribution process. Typically, at the beginning of the marketing period (often a month), product issuers create a suite of reverse convertibles that will be issued at the end of the month. The issuer fixes all of the characteristics of each reverse convertible, including the broker fee, at the beginning of each month.16Over the course of the month, issuers market available reverse convertibles to brokers, who then solicit orders from retail consumers. At the end of the month, all of the orders are accumulated and the reverse convertible is issued such that demand is completely satisfied. In other words, all of the product characteristics are set in advance and the supply curve is completely elastic. Issuers sell the reverse convertibles to brokers at a fixed par price of 100% minus the fixed broker fee. Brokers then sell reverse convertibles to the end consumer at a fixed price of par (100%).17For each product sold, brokers earn the broker's fee. Since issuers pay the fee, it represents a transfer from the issuer to the broker. Consequently, consumers are indifferent to the broker fee conditional on the risk and return of the product.
Issuers sell reverse convertibles through brokers rather than selling them directly to consumers. Reverse convertible issuers are almost exclusively investment banks, and thus they typically underwrite their own reverse convertible bonds. The investment banks that issue reverse convertibles could theoretically market reverse convertibles on a wide-scale basis directly to consumers, as often done in the mutual fund industry with mutual funds. However, SEC regulations such as the Securities Act of 1933 restrict the marketing of financial products to end consumers. Any materials used to market an SEC-registered security (such as the reverse convertibles studied here) must be vetted for legal and compliance reasons and formally filed with the SEC. Since creating marketing materials can be a costly and lengthy process relative to the marketing period (often one month), issuers do not market reverse convertibles directly to consumers.18Rather, issuers choose to sell reverse convertibles to brokers who market them to consumers.
The empirical analysis uses a new hand-collected reverse convertible bond data set. The data set covers U.S. SEC-registered one-year maturity reverse convertibles issued over the period 2008 to 2012. Issuance data, specifically the date, coupon, and size details, are from Bloomberg and the Mergent Fixed Income Securities Database. Details on each reverse convertible's broker commissions, initial equity share price, and convertible trigger price are manually collected from the corresponding Form 424B filings found on the SEC EDGARS website. The data set is supplemented with equity volatility data from Option Metrics and Credit Default Swap (CDS) data from Markit.
TableIdisplays summary statistics for the data set. The mean and median issuance size in the sample was $1.64 million and $665 thousand, respectively.19On average, reverse convertibles paid a coupon of 10.50% per annum. The option premium measures the value of the put options embedded in each reverse convertible expressed as a percentage of the notional invested.20The one-year CDS spread reflects the default risk for senior unsecured debt, which corresponds to the issuer credit risk inherent to each reverse convertible bond.
Reverse convertibles are issued almost exclusively by banks. Five banks—ABN Amro, Barclays Bank, JPMorgan Chase & Co, UBS, and Royal Bank of Canada—dominate the issuance market for one-year reverse convertibles, making up over 80% of the market over the period 2008 to 2012. Apple Inc. served as the most popular underlying equity to link the principal payment of a reverse convertible to. Other popular underlying equities included Bank of America Corporation, General Electric Company, Caterpillar Inc., and JPMorgan Chase & Co.
article.II. Empirical Analysis:
One of the most striking features of the reverse convertible bond market is that there is substantial heterogeneity in the value of convertible bonds from the consumer's perspective. Similarly, there is substantial heterogeneity in the fees/kick-backs paid to brokers. In this section, I document the heterogeneity in returns and systematically examine which convertible bonds consumers purchase.
Figure3, Panel A, plots the dispersion in the implied value of reverse convertible bonds. The standard deviation of implied value is over 2.40% relative to the notional invested. Relative to the average risk-free rate over the period studied (0.60%), the dispersion in implied value is substantial.
In the data set of 3,066 reverse convertible bonds, 143 of the reverse convertibles either unambiguously dominate or are dominated by another reverse convertible. By “unambiguously dominated,” I am referring to two literally identical reverse convertible bonds issued on the same date and sold for the same price with the only difference being the bond coupon. Essentially 1 in 10 markets studied contains a dominated product. Figure3, Panel B, displays the dispersion in terms of the implied value for each convertible bond among the set of dominated and superior bonds. On average, the coupon and subsequent return of the superior convertible bond is 1.60 percentage points higher than the corresponding dominated convertible bond.21If I were to broaden the definition of dominated products in terms of the implied value, then essentially every market, defined based on the issuance month and underlying equity, contains a clearly dominant or dominated product. On average, the implied value of the best product is 2.08% (relative to the notional invested) higher than the worst product available in a market.
Given that there is substantial heterogeneity in implied value, which convertible bonds are consumers buying? One would expect issuance size to be increasing in the implied value since the product characteristics are set in advance and the supply curve is completely elastic (as discussed in SectionI.B). Figure4, Panels A and B, displays binned scatter plots of the implied value of a reverse convertible versus the quantity issued. The results display an inverted demand curve. Consumers actually buy more of the worse products. This is true when we look at all reverse convertibles (Figure4, Panel A) as well as just the set of superior/dominated reverse convertibles (Figure4, Panel B).
Previous research emphasizes the importance of search and heterogeneity in consumer sophistication in financial markets. A random search model can help explain why consumers with high search costs or a lower level of sophistication might buy inferior products but cannot help explain why consumers purchase more of these products. A standard random search model would predict the exact opposite, that is, that consumers should purchase more of the superior product.
The incentives of intermediaries help rationalize the findings from SectionII.A.1that consumers purchase inferior products and purchase more of them. Product issuers compensate brokers for selling products by paying brokers a fee/kick-back for each product sold. Figure5, Panel A, plots the dispersion in fees paid to brokers. The fees paid to brokers range from under 0.50% to upward of 5.00% relative to the notional invested. Brokers can earn over a 10× higher commission for selling the products with the highest broker fees relative to the products with the lowest broker fees. The heterogeneity in broker fees illustrates that brokers have economically important incentives to sell certain products over others.
How do broker fees relate to product quality? Figure5, Panel B, displays a binned scatter plot of broker fees and implied value. As can be seen, there is a strong negative and statistically significant relationship between broker fees and the implied value of a reverse convertible bond. The results indicate that brokers are incentivized to sell inferior products.
I next reexamine which convertible bonds consumers purchase, accounting for differences in broker fees/kick-backs. Figure6, Panels A and B, displays binned scatter plots of the implied value of a convertible bond versus the quantity issued, in which I control for differences in broker fees. Once I account for differences in broker fees, I find a positive and significant relationship between the quantity of convertible bonds demanded and implied value. The results suggest that accounting for broker fees is of first-order importance in understanding demand for convertible bonds. Any attempt to understand investment flows without observing the kick-backs paid to brokers would be hollow. Demand for reverse convertibles is jointly determined by consumers and brokers.
TableIIdisplays the regression estimates corresponding to equation2. Columns (1) to (4) include the results for the full data set, whereas columns (5) and (6) display the regression results corresponding to when the data set is restricted to the set of dominated/superior products. The relevant coefficients not only have the expected sign, but also are statistically significantly different from zero. As expected, issuance size is positively correlated with coupon and negatively correlated with equity option premium and issuer credit risk. The results from column (3) indicate that a one-percentage-point increase in coupon is associated with a $110,800 increase in issue size. Similarly, a one-percentage-point increase in equity put option premium is correlated with a $61,800 decrease in issue size, whereas a one-percentage-point increase in issuer credit risk (CDS spread) is correlated with a $508,900 decrease in issue size. The results suggest that consumers do a reasonably good job of valuing reverse convertible bonds. All else equal, consumers buy more of convertibles that are less risky and have higher coupons.
The regression results also indicate that demand is increasing in broker fees. In each specification, I estimate a positive and significant relationship between broker fees and issue size, even when I restrict the data set to the set of superior/dominated products. The results from column (1) indicate that a one-percentage-point increase in broker fees is correlated with a $447,200 increase in issue size. Recall that, conditional on the risk and return of a product, consumers should be indifferent to broker fees. One might be concerned that some omitted product characteristic that is positively correlated with fees and size may be driving this relationship. However, I am able to control for all product characteristics, especially when I restrict the data set to the set of dominated/superior products. These results suggest that brokers are more inclined to sell high-fee products.
TableIIIdisplays the estimation results. The columns differ in terms of the covariates that are included and the data set that is used. The results indicate that fees are negatively correlated with product coupon and are positively correlated with equity option premium and issuer credit risk. The estimated coupon coefficients in all three specifications are negative and significant at the 1% level. The estimates indicate that a one-percentage-point increase in coupon is associated with a 10 bp decrease in broker fees. Similarly, a one-percentage-point increase in option premium is correlated with a 7 bp increase in broker fees. The estimates from column (2) imply that a one-percentage-point increase in the issuer's CDS spread (issuer credit risk) is correlated with a 6 bp increase in broker fees. To put these estimates into perspective, the average level of fees in the data set is 2.24%.
Overall, the results from the empirical analysis confirm that the incentives of brokers do not align with the incentives of consumers. All else equal, consumers are more likely to buy reverse convertibles with high broker fees. However, reverse convertibles with high fees tend to have worse payoffs. In this sense, brokers are incentivized to sell consumers inferior products.
article.III. A Model of Financial Product Distri:
The heterogeneity in the value of reverse convertible bonds raises several questions. Why do consumers buy inferior convertibles? Second, why do brokers sell both inferior and superior convertibles? And third, why are consumers more likely to purchase inferior products? In this section, I develop a model of financial distribution that rationalizes the behavior of consumers and brokers. I then structurally estimate the model in order to analyze and quantify the underlying economic frictions in the marketplace.
In the model, consumers search for financial products with the aid of a broker. Costly search/heterogeneous consumer sophistication prevents consumers from purchasing the best available financial products. The model and estimation are in the spirit of the search models developed in Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004) and Hong and Shum (2006). The key innovation in the model is the role of brokers/financial intermediaries. In the model, brokers endogenously direct the search of consumers based on the broker's profit motives, and brokers price discriminate across consumers based on the consumer's level of financial sophistication. These model features help explain the existence of dominated financial products and, more importantly, why consumers purchase more of the dominated product. Brokers use dominated/superior products as a way to price discriminate across consumers, selling high-fee dominated products to unsophisticated consumers and low-fee superior products to sophisticated consumers.
I develop a model in which consumers search for reverse convertible bonds with the aid of brokers. The model involves three types of market participants: consumers, brokers, and financial product issuers. Although applicable to most retail financial products, the model is tailored to the distribution of reverse convertible bonds. The model is outlined as follows. Product issuers create a suite of available convertible bonds that are characterized by their payoffc(coupon), short equity put premiume, product issuer credit riskd, and broker feef. The actions of issuers and the set of available reverse convertibles are taken as given and are outside the model.23Rather, the model focuses on the endogenous interactions between brokers and consumers, taking the product space as given. Consumers possess demand for one convertible bond and value each bond based on its risk and return. Consumers sequentially search over the product space one product at a time with the aid of a broker. Consumers are only able to access the convertible bond market with the aid of a broker. Each period, a consumer is matched with exactly one broker. Brokers service their consumer client base by directing a consumer's search and selecting one product to offer each client from the set of available convertible bonds. Each broker selects the convertible bond to offer to each consumer client that maximizes the broker's expected profit given the product-specific broker fee and the consumer's level of financial sophistication. Consumers can either purchase the bond offered or continue searching for a new investment opportunity next period. If the consumer decides to continue searching, she is matched with a new broker and is offered a new product in the following period. The key feature of the model is the broker-intermediated demand system.
Neither the price of a reverse convertible nor the broker fee enters the consumer's utility function. This is because all reverse convertibles are sold at a fixed price of par (100%) and the broker fee is paid by the product issuer rather than the consumer. Conditional on the risk and return of a product, consumers are indifferent to the broker fee. This feature of the market structure is critical for the empirical analysis, as it provides a way to separately identify the preferences of consumers and brokers.
Each consumer is matched with exactly one broker. Brokers are assumed to be ex ante identical and thus I abstract from the broker/consumer matching process. Brokers market exactly one convertible bond to each consumer. In other words, brokers form a consumer's consideration set, which consists of exactly one convertible bond. Consumers can either purchase or forgo purchasing the convertible bond that is offered. If consumers purchase convertible bondjthat is offered, they receive utility flowuj, whereas if they forgo the offered product, they will continue searching for a new product.
Costly search prevents consumers from simply searching across all products and purchasing the product that yields the highest utility. There are two types of consumers: sophisticated consumers (searchers) and unsophisticated consumers (nonsearchers). By definition, unsophisticated consumers have sufficiently high search costs such that they always purchase the first product offered, while sophisticated consumers may search across the product space. One could also interpret unsophisticated consumers as being unable to discern the quality of a convertible bond and thus they always purchase the offered bond.24The fraction of sophisticated consumers in the population is denoted byωS. Each period, sophisticated consumers must pay a search costviin order to observe a new product offer from a broker. Search costs are heterogeneous across sophisticated consumers and are distributedvi∼F(·). Unsophisticated investors face prohibitively high search costs such that they never search across products. Consumer types (sophisticated/unsophisticated) reflect the information observed by brokers. Brokers observe a consumer's type and preferences but not the consumer's exact search cost. Thus, brokers have incomplete information regarding the exact level of a consumer's financial sophistication. As shown in the proceeding section, brokers price discriminate across sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers by selecting different products to market to each type of consumer.
Brokers act as a liaison between consumers and financial product issuers. Brokers observe the full scope of available convertible bonds and select one bond to offer each of their consumer clients. If the consumer purchases the bond, the product issuer pays the corresponding broker a product-specific fee. Feesfjfor a given bondjare fixed by the product issuer but are heterogeneous across bonds.
I study a pure-strategy Bayes Nash equilibrium. I focus on a stationary equilibrium in which the distribution of offered products and consumer types is constant over time. Alternatively, one could think of the market as clearing instantaneously. The equilibrium is characterized by the optimal behavior of consumers and brokers. Consumers maximize utility by optimally searching across the product space. Consumers are fully rational and have correct beliefs about the distribution of available convertible bonds shown to sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers in terms of the indirect utilities,u∼HS(·)andu∼HU(·). Brokers market to a consumer the convertible bond that maximizes the broker's expected profits given the consumer's type, preferences, and utility maximization decision. Details on the equilibrium and optimal strategy of each participant are discussed in detail in the proceeding subsections.
Sophisticated consumers sequentially search for the optimal investment among the discrete product space{u1,u2,…un}. Products are numbered such thatuj≤uj+1,∀j. While searching, a consumer receives an offer from a broker each period and then chooses to either purchase the offered bond or continue searching. If the consumer decides to continue searching, she pays a search costviand receives an offer from a new broker in the subsequent period. All subsequent product offers are drawn i.i.d. from either the stationary distributionHS(·)or the stationary distributionHU(·), depending on whether the consumer is a sophisticated or unsophisticated consumer. As I discuss below, the key innovation in the model is that the distributions of products observed by sophisticated and unsophisticated consumersHS(·)andHU(·)are endogenously determined in equilibrium based on the incentives of brokers.
Unsophisticated consumers select the first product offered to them by brokers because they face prohibitively high search costs. The probability that an unsophisticated consumer observes a particular productjis denoted byρj,Uand is endogenously determined based on broker profit maximization. The set of probabilitiesρ1,U,ρ2,U,…,ρn,Uform the distribution of products offered,HU(·). Since brokers observe a consumer's type, the distributions of products offeredHS(·)andHU(·)likely vary across types in equilibrium.
In equilibrium, consumer beliefs aboutHS(·)andHU(·)are correct and completely rational.30Unsophisticated consumers have sufficiently high search costs that it is always optimal to purchase the first product offered. Sophisticated consumers optimally search by adopting a reservation utility strategy that is a weakly decreasing function of their search costvigiven the distribution of available productsHS(·).
A broker's profit function, and consequently the probability that he shows a particular convertible bond to a consumer, depends on the consumer's type. Because brokers observe a consumer's type, brokers price discriminate across the two types of consumers in equilibrium. Unsophisticated consumers are more likely offered the purely dominated products, while sophisticated consumers are more likely offered the superior products.
article.IV. Model Estimation:
The model of financial distribution developed in SectionIIIlends itself to structural estimation. Using the reverse convertible bond data set, I structurally estimate the underlying model parameters. The key parameters of interest are consumer preferences, the broker's profit function, and the distribution of consumer types.
To facilitate estimation I assume that consumers employ the same set of reservation strategies across all markets. In other words,G(·)is assumed to be constant across all markets. This assumption is equivalent to assuming that the distribution of search costs, consumer types, and consumer beliefs overHS(·)andHU(·)are constant across markets. For example, this implies that consumers searching for Apple-linked reverse convertibles and Microsoft-linked reverse convertibles hold the same beliefs over the distribution of available reverse convertibles. This assumption provides additional statistical power to estimateG(·); otherwise, it would have to be separately estimated for each market. Although this assumption restricts consumer beliefs, it is plausible that consumers searching for Apple- or Microsoft-linked reverse convertibles employ the same search strategy.
The model is parameterized as follows. The utility function is specified as a linear function of coupon, option premium, and the CDS spread according to equation4. The utility formulation of the model allows for one normalization. Due to its arbitrary scale, I normalize consumer preferences for coupon equal to one. Under this normalization, the remaining utility parameters can be interpreted in terms of monetary value or percentage return. I also parameterize the broker's profit function by including product issuer fixed effects for the four largest issuers: ABN Amro, Barclays, JPMorgan, and UBS. For example, it may be less costly for brokers to sell products issued by JPMorgan Chase, or brokers may simply prefer to sell JPMorgan Chase products because of a preexisting relationship. The SEC has recently investigated brokers for steering clients to particular product issuers because of preexisting relationships.33Similarly, evidence in Foerster et al. (2017) suggests that broker preferences may be more important than consumer preferences when determining portfolios. For these reasons, I include fixed effects in the broker profit function.
Estimation of the model requires no additional assumptions regarding the parametric form of the distribution of reservation utilitiesG(·). Following Barseghyan et al. (2013), I flexibly estimate the distribution functionG(·)using a third-order polynomial approximation tologG(·). I estimate polynomial approximations tologG(·)rather thanG(·)to ensure that the estimated distributionG(·)̂is strictly positive. However, I do not restrict the estimated distribution functions to be weakly increasing. Variation in the data helps identify the curvature of the reservation utility functionsG(·). Note that the scale ofG(·)is not separately identified fromθSin the above conditional likelihood. Rather, the scale ofG(·)is pinned down by the fact that all consumers purchase the best product that yields utilityu¯. In other words, no consumer continues searching if she observes the best available product. Hence,G(u¯)=1.34
The underlying data and model separately identify the consumer utility and broker parameters as well as the observed distribution of consumer types. Although each parameter of the model is jointly identified through the data, I provide a brief stylized discussion of the intuition behind the identification of the key parameters of the model. The preference parametersγOptandγCDSmeasure how consumers trade off option premium and issuer credit risk (CDS) relative to the coupon. Identification of preferences is best illustrated through the following thought experiment. Suppose we observe a product with feesf, couponc, and equity option premiumethat has market shares. Now suppose that we decrease the coupon fromctoc′, wherec′<c. The question we are interested in is how much the option premium would have to decrease (frometoe′) to keep the market share of the product unchanged ats. The compensating change in option premium identifies how consumers trade off option premium for coupon.
Intuitively, identification of the distribution ofG(·)closely follows that of the preference parameters. The conceptual experiment we would like to be able to run is to freely vary the coupon of a product and see how the corresponding product's market share changes, keeping all other products and product characteristics constant. Such variation would allow us to trace out the curvature ofG(·).
The variation in consumer types is identified by variation in the distribution of product offerings across markets. Specifically, the variation in substitution patterns across markets identifies consumer types. Consider a market that consists of one clearly superior high-utility, low-fee bond and one dominated low-utility, high-fee bond. Now suppose that an additional inferior bond is introduced into the market. We can identify the proportion of sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers(ωS)based on how the market share of the superior bond changes when an additional inferior bond is introduced into the market. If the market share of the superior bond falls dramatically, this would suggest that those consumers who initially purchased the superior reverse convertible were “lucky” unsophisticated consumers. In context, if the market share of the superior bond does not change much, this would suggest that those consumers who initially purchased the reverse convertible were primarily sophisticated consumers. Variation in substitution patterns across markets is the key feature of the data that identifies consumer types.
The maximum likelihood estimates are reported in TableIV. In column (1), I estimate the model under the assumption that all consumers are sophisticated investors(ωS=1). In column (2), I estimate the model allowing for sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers. As expected, the results indicate that consumer utility is decreasing in equity option premium and issuer credit risk (CDS). In all specifications, I estimate a negative and statistically significant relationship between utility and the two measures of risk. The results from column (1) indicate that consumers are indifferent between a one-percentage-point increase in coupon and a 0.66-percentage-point decrease in option premium. Similarly, consumers are willing to trade off a one-percentage-point increase in coupon for a 4.18-percentage-point decrease in the corresponding CDS spread. Recall that, under a risk-neutral framework, consumers should be willing to trade off option premium and CDS spread roughly one-for-one with coupon. Similar to the reduced-form results in SectionII.B, it appears that consumers do a fairly good job of valuing reverse convertible bonds but are particularly sensitive to issuer credit risk.
In the two-consumer-type model, I also estimate the distribution of consumer typesωS. The estimates in column (2) suggest that 74.50% of the population consists of sophisticated consumers while the remaining 25.50% of the population consists of unsophisticated consumers. These results suggest that brokers have the ability to price discriminate across consumer types.
I parameterize the broker's profit function to include fixed effects for the four largest issuers. In general, the fixed effects across the four issuers are statistically indistinguishable except for Barclays Capital. The estimates suggest that brokers are twice as likely to market a Barclays-issued convertible bond as an otherwise-identical Goldman Sachs convertible bond. The results are consistent with Barclays being namedRisk Magazine's Structured Product House of the Year in 2009.
The structural model provides additional quantitative insight into the forces driving the market for reverse convertibles. The empirical evidence suggests that costly search prevents consumers from finding the superior reverse convertibles in the market. Using the model estimates of the distribution of reservation utilitiesG(·)̂and the observed distribution of available productsHS(·), I am able to recover the distribution of search costs by inverting equation7as described in the Appendix. Recovering the distribution of search costs allows us to quantitatively assess the results and understand the economics of the market.
Figure7, Panel A, displays the estimated distribution of search costs for the two-consumer-type model. The estimated search costs suggest that roughly 50% of sophisticated consumers have search costs below 150 bp and that the average search cost is 140 bp relative to the notional invested. In other words, over 50% of sophisticated consumers behave as if the cost (in terms of time value of money and effort) of soliciting an additional offer from a broker is less than $15 for a $1,000 investment. To put these numbers into perspective, Hall and Woodward (2012) find in the mortgage market that the median consumer behaves as if the cost of soliciting an additional mortgage broker is roughly 100 bp relative to the mortgage principal, while Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004) find in the S&P load fund market that the median consumer behaves as if the cost of searching is roughly 25 bp relative to the notional invested.35Given the differences in access and transparency across these markets, the differences in search costs are reasonable.
The estimates also provide insight into the expected number of searches a consumer makes. The probability that a sophisticated consumer with reservation utilityuris shown a product that exceeds her reservation utility is1−HS(ur). Hence, the expected number of searches a consumer makes is11−HS(uR). Figure7, Panel B, displays the implied distribution of searches. The estimates indicate that, on average, most consumers do very little searching, which is consistent with intuition. Roughly, 80% of consumers expect to either purchase the first product offered or make one additional search. The results also suggest that those consumers that do search tend to do so until they find the best available products. Given the distribution of search costs, the average consumer spends $30, in terms of total search expenditures, in searching for a $1,000 investment.
We can also use the estimates to pin down the hypothetical search costs of the nonsearching unsophisticated consumers. A consumer who never searches behaves as if the cost of soliciting an additional offer from a broker is roughly $70 for a $1,000 investment. Alternatively, a sophisticated consumer with search costs above $25 for a $1,000 investment would expect to make no additional searches in equilibrium (E[Searches]<0.5). Overall, the estimates indicate that reasonably small search frictions can rationalize the behavior of sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers in the data.
The structural estimates also help us assess the degree of price discrimination occurring in the reverse convertible market. Consider the hypothetical market that comprises two nearly identical convertible bonds where the payout of one bond dominates the payout of the other. The superior convertible bond pays a coupon of 12% and a broker fee of 1.00%. The dominated convertible bond pays a coupon of 8% and a broker fee of 5.00%. We can use the parameter estimates to determine the probability that consumers of each type observe each convertible bond as displayed in TableV. Brokers are equally likely to offer the superior bond relative to the dominated bond to a sophisticated consumer. However, brokers are more than twice as likely to offer the dominated bond as the superior bond to an unsophisticated consumer. Brokers do not always offer the dominated bond to an unsophisticated consumer because of the cost shockηijUin the broker profit function, which reflects broker heterogeneity and/or mistakes. Overall, these results help explain not only why consumers buy dominated convertible bonds, but also why consumers actually purchase more of the dominated convertible bonds than the superior convertible bonds.
This simple example illustrates why the conflict of interest problem compounds the consumer search problem. Sophisticated (low-search-cost) consumers are more likely to observe better products when they search for a product, and thus if a sophisticated consumer observes a bad product, she will continue searching. In other words, sophisticated consumers can overcome the conflict of interest problem. The conflict of interest problem is more problematic for unsophisticated (high-search-cost) consumers, as unsophisticated consumers are more likely to observe inferior products and hence are less likely to continue searching.
article.V. Understanding the Role of Brokers in:
Two economic forces/frictions appear to drive the existence and prevalence of dominated products. First, consumers must not be aware of or able to purchase the superior product. I model the consumer's problem as fundamentally a search problem. Second, the consumer's search problem is confounded by the fact that the incentives of brokers do not align with the incentives of consumers. As consumers search for new investment products, they are more likely to see high-fee products. The conflict of interest problem burdens consumers with excess search. The structural estimation results provide a way of quantifying the role that brokers play in the consumer search process and illustrate how the conflict of interest problem exacerbates the costs associated with search/informational frictions in retail financial markets.
Both of these counterfactuals change the distribution of products observed by consumers,HS(·)andHU(·). Given the distribution of available products and the distribution of search costs observed in the data, I can calculate the updated reservation utility according to equation7. A feature of the search model is that the consumer's expected return from entering the market is equal simply to her reservation utility. By comparing the change in consumer reservation utilities, I can examine how consumer returns change across the two counterfactuals.
I first examine the counterfactual in which consumers randomly sample the product space. This counterfactual can loosely be thought of as a world in which brokers potentially help consumers access markets but do not direct consumers' search. TableVIdisplays the corresponding estimates. The expected return of sophisticated consumers increases by 5 bp. The policy change does not have a large impact on sophisticated consumers because, although brokers were previously incentivized to show sophisticated consumers high-fee products, they were also incentivized to show good products that a sophisticated consumer might actually buy. The fact that sophisticated consumers benefit from this policy only marginally is not surprising given the results discussed in SectionIV.B.2. When both a dominated and a superior product are available in the example given in SectionIV.B.2, sophisticated consumers observe the products with equal probability (TableV). Unsophisticated consumers benefit from the policy substantially more as their expected return increases by 21 bp. The conflict of interest problem is more costly for unsophisticated consumers because brokers know that unsophisticated consumers purchase any offered product and do not search. This example illustrates how the conflict of interest problem interacts with search costs and consumer sophistication.
The second counterfactual examines consumer investment outcomes if brokers were forced to show the best available product in a market. This counterfactual implicitly assumes that regulators are able to perfectly align the incentives of brokers and consumers. In this counterfactual, brokers help consumers search across the product space more efficiently by directing consumer search toward the best available products. TableVIdisplays the corresponding results. The results suggest that both sophisticated and unsophisticated consumers benefit substantially from the policy change. The expected risk-adjusted returns of sophisticated consumers and unsophisticated consumers increase by 95 and 120 bp, respectively. Relative to the risk-free rate over this period (0.60%), these gains are substantial. The reason for such gains is that aligning the incentives of brokers and consumers helps resolve the search frictions present in this market—consumer search is directed to the best available products.
Comparing the results of the two counterfactuals offers additional insight into the frictions in these markets. The consumer gains in the random search counterfactual are relatively modest compared to the consumer gains in the optimally directed search counterfactual. The reason the gains are smaller in the random search counterfactual is that, although the random search counterfactual resolves the conflict of interest problem, it does not resolve the fundamental friction in this market, namely, costly search. In context, aligning the incentives of brokers and consumers in the optimally directed search counterfactual alleviates the search frictions in this market.
These counterfactuals help shed light on some of the new regulatory proposals regarding consumer financial products. Regulators worldwide are increasingly attempting to address the asymmetry between broker and consumer incentives.36The optimally directed search counterfactual provides insight into a “best-case” scenario where regulators are able to perfectly solve the conflict of interest problem, and illustrates how brokers could alleviate information/search frictions in retail financial markets. The random search counterfactual reflects a scenario in which regulators are able to solve the conflict of interest problem but one where brokers have no incentives to help consumers search. An important caveat is that both counterfactuals reflect a partial-equilibrium analysis in which the product space is held fixed. The product space will likely evolve with any new policy changes.37Overall, the results suggest that improving consumer search, either by lowering search costs/improving sophistication or aligning the incentives of brokers and consumers, can improve outcomes for consumers. The results also suggest that the search and incentive asymmetries inherent to these markets will potentially be difficult to resolve.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Economists and regulators have long been interested in the observed price dispersion in financial products. Does such price dispersion imply that consumers are overpaying for investments? Using a new data set, I find evidence that consumers often purchase products with dominated payoff structures. Even more alarmingly, when both a superior and a dominated product are available, consumers are more likely to end up with the latter.
Previous research has highlighted consumer search as the mechanism behind price heterogeneity and potentially dominated financial products. Search and information frictions help explain why consumers buy dominated products, but are not sufficient to explain why consumers are more likely to purchase dominated products over superior products. I argue that consumers are more likely to purchase dominated products because the product fee structure incentivizes brokers to sell dominated products, that is, the incentives of brokers differ from the incentives of consumers. The empirical evidence is consistent with such incentive asymmetry. All else equal, consumers are more likely to buy products with higher fees. Moreover, all else equal, products with higher fees have lower payoffs. Thus, the incentive asymmetry between brokers and consumers compounds the consumer search problem and burdens consumers with excess search.
The findings that consumers frequently overpay for investments and that the incentives of brokers do not align with those of consumers are likely not unique to the reverse convertible industry. This paper focuses on reverse convertible bonds because some features of the reverse convertible bond market make identifying dominated products and the incentives of brokers easier. There is little reason to believe that search and broker incentives do not play important roles in other financial markets. A vast literature examines price heterogeneity in financial markets and suggests that consumers might be overpaying for investments in other product markets (Hortaçsu and Syverson (2004), Green, Hollifield, and Schurhoff (2007), Gurun, Matvos, and Seru (2016)). Similarly, previous works, such as Livingston and O'Neal (1996), Mahoney (2004), Bergsteresser, Chalmers, and Tufano (2009), Christoffersen, Evans, and Musto (2013), and Del Guercio and Reuter (2014), detail the potential conflict of interest arising in the mutual fund industry. Thus, the presence of dominated products and the broker/consumer incentive asymmetry prevalent in the market for reverse convertibles are more likely to be closer to the rule than the exception in retail financial markets.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
